network.overall_strategy: This network generates valine through three independent terminal routes—Strecker synthesis, reductive amination, and transamination—all converging from prebiotically plausible C1-C4 building blocks. The strategy avoids the speculative acetolactate pathway entirely, instead using Varma et al.-type reductive carboxylation of isobutyraldehyde to generate α-ketoisovalerate. Hydrothermal environments produce C1 species (CO, formate, formaldehyde) and small aldehydes (acetaldehyde, propanal) via Fischer-Tropsch-type chemistry on mineral catalysts. Surface environments drive aldol condensation, HCN synthesis, and Strecker chemistry. Biochemical environments enable transamination and final macromolecular-level assembly. Hub molecules (formaldehyde, HCN, isobutyraldehyde, α-ketoisovalerate, pyruvate) create genuine convergence points where multiple independent routes intersect, providing redundancy and robustness.

reactions.name: CO₂ reduction to CO via reverse water-gas shift on magnetite

conditions.other: serpentinization-derived H₂

reactions.mechanism: Reverse water-gas shift reaction: CO₂ + H₂ → CO + H₂O catalyzed on magnetite surfaces at hydrothermal temperatures. CO₂ adsorbs on Fe³⁺ sites and is reduced by H₂ dissociated on the mineral surface.
reactions.reasoning: Magnetite-catalyzed reverse water-gas shift is well-established in hydrothermal experiments. Seewald et al. (2006) demonstrated CO₂-CO interconversion under hydrothermal conditions. Magnetite is a known catalyst for this reaction at 400-500K (McCollom & Seewald, 2007). CO is a critical C1 feedstock for subsequent Fischer-Tropsch-type chemistry.

reactions.name: CO₂ reduction to formate on greigite

conditions.other: alkaline vent conditions, pH 9-11

reactions.mechanism: CO₂ is adsorbed onto the greigite surface, which acts as an analog of biological [Fe₄S₄] clusters. Hydride transfer from surface-bound H₂ reduces CO₂ to formate (HCOO⁻) via a two-electron reduction.
reactions.reasoning: Roldan et al. (2015) demonstrated that greigite (Fe₃S₄) reduces CO₂ to formate under mild hydrothermal conditions, drawing the analogy between greigite and the [Fe₄S₄] clusters in carbon-fixing enzymes like CO dehydrogenase/acetyl-CoA synthase. Herschy et al. (2014) confirmed formate production in simulated alkaline vent conditions.

reactions.name: Fischer-Tropsch-type formaldehyde synthesis from CO

conditions.other: FTT synthesis regime, CO:H₂ ≈ 1:2

reactions.mechanism: CO hydrogenation on magnetite surface via Fischer-Tropsch-type mechanism. CO adsorbs on iron surface, H₂ dissociates, and stepwise hydrogenation yields formaldehyde (the C1 oxygenate product). Partial hydrogenation is favored at lower residence times.
reactions.reasoning: McCollom et al. (1999) and McCollom & Seewald (2006) demonstrated FTT synthesis of small oxygenates including formaldehyde on magnetite surfaces under hydrothermal conditions. Formaldehyde is the simplest aldehyde product and serves as a C1 building block for subsequent aldol chemistry.

reactions.name: Formate reduction to formaldehyde on native iron

conditions.other: mildly alkaline, reducing conditions

reactions.mechanism: Two-electron reduction of formate on native iron surface. Fe⁰ acts as electron donor, reducing the carboxylate group of formate to the aldehyde oxidation state, yielding formaldehyde. Fe⁰ is oxidized to Fe²⁺.
reactions.reasoning: Varma et al. (2018) showed that native iron (Fe⁰) in aqueous conditions acts as a potent reductant capable of multi-electron reductions of C1 species. The reduction of formate to formaldehyde is thermodynamically favorable with Fe⁰ as reductant under mildly alkaline hydrothermal conditions. This provides an alternative formaldehyde source independent of CO-based FTT.

reactions.name: Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis of C2-C3 aldehydes

conditions.other: FTT regime with chain growth, Anderson-Schulz-Flory distribution

reactions.mechanism: Fischer-Tropsch-type chain growth on magnetite surface. CO and H₂ undergo repeated C1 insertion and hydrogenation steps. Chain termination via oxygenate pathway yields linear aldehydes: acetaldehyde (C2) and propanal (C3) as products following Anderson-Schulz-Flory distribution.
reactions.reasoning: McCollom et al. (1999) demonstrated FTT synthesis of linear C1-C5 oxygenates (alcohols, aldehydes) on magnetite catalysts under hydrothermal conditions. Acetaldehyde and propanal are well-established FTT products with reasonable yields. Rushdi & Simoneit (2001) confirmed the formation of C2-C4 oxygenates in FTT experiments.

reactions.name: Photochemical HCN synthesis from NH₃ and formaldehyde

conditions.other: UV irradiation (λ < 300 nm), no ozone layer, gas-phase or thin film

reactions.mechanism: UV photolysis of formaldehyde generates radical intermediates. TiO₂ photocatalysis generates reactive oxygen species and surface radicals. Ammonia reacts with formaldehyde-derived radicals (·CHO, ·CH₂OH) on TiO₂ surface to form aminomethanol, which dehydrates to formaldimine (CH₂=NH), then further dehydrogenation under UV yields HCN.
reactions.reasoning: Ferris & Hagan (1984) demonstrated HCN production from NH₃ and CH₂O under UV irradiation. TiO₂ photocatalysis enhances this reaction (Ritson & Sutherland, 2012). HCN is also produced volcanically, but this synthetic route from prebiotically available HCHO and NH₃ ensures the network is self-contained. HCN is essential for Strecker synthesis.

reactions.name: Cross-aldol condensation of propanal with formaldehyde

conditions.other: mildly alkaline pH 8-10, wet-dry cycling promotes concentration

reactions.mechanism: Base-catalyzed cross-aldol condensation. The alpha-proton of propanal is abstracted by basic sites on montmorillonite. The resulting enolate attacks the carbonyl of formaldehyde to give 2-(hydroxymethyl)propanal. Subsequent dehydration (promoted by dry phase of wet-dry cycle) yields methacrolein (2-methylprop-2-enal).
reactions.reasoning: Aldol condensations between small aldehydes are well-established in prebiotic chemistry. Weber (1992, 2001) demonstrated sugar synthesis via aldol reactions under prebiotic conditions. Montmorillonite catalyzes aldol reactions through its Brønsted acid/base sites and concentrates organics via adsorption (Ferris, 2005). Cross-aldol of propanal with formaldehyde is thermodynamically favored because formaldehyde has no alpha-H and acts exclusively as the electrophile.

reactions.name: Reduction of methacrolein to isobutyraldehyde on pyrite

conditions.other: FeS as electron donor, mildly acidic to neutral pH

reactions.mechanism: Selective hydrogenation of the C=C double bond in methacrolein while preserving the aldehyde group. FeS/FeS₂ surfaces donate electrons for the reduction. H₂ dissociates on pyrite surface providing H atoms for 1,4-conjugate addition across the α,β-unsaturated aldehyde system, yielding the saturated isobutyraldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Pyrite and mackinawite surfaces are known to catalyze reductions of organic molecules. Wächtershäuser (1988, 1992) proposed FeS/FeS₂ as primordial reducing agents. Cody et al. (2004) demonstrated that metal sulfide surfaces can selectively reduce unsaturated organic compounds. The selective 1,4-reduction of an α,β-unsaturated aldehyde to a saturated aldehyde is thermodynamically favorable and well-established in heterogeneous catalysis.

reactions.name: Direct FTT synthesis of isobutyraldehyde

conditions.other: FTT conditions favoring branching, higher temperature regime

reactions.mechanism: Fischer-Tropsch-type chain growth with branch formation. At higher temperatures, secondary carbon radical intermediates on the catalyst surface undergo β-hydride elimination and re-insertion, creating branched products. Chain termination by CO insertion and hydrogenation at the branch point yields 2-methylpropanal (isobutyraldehyde) as a minor branched C4 product.
reactions.reasoning: While FTT primarily yields linear products following Anderson-Schulz-Flory distribution, branched products including isobutyraldehyde have been detected in FTT experiments at elevated temperatures (Rushdi & Simoneit, 2001). The branching fraction increases with temperature. Yields are low (typically <5% of C4 fraction is branched), but this provides an alternative hydrothermal route to isobutyraldehyde independent of the aldol pathway. McCollom & Seewald (2007) note that FTT product distributions in hydrothermal systems include branched oxygenates.

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis step 1: aminonitrile formation from isobutyraldehyde

conditions.other: mildly alkaline pH 8-9, concentrated evaporitic pool

reactions.mechanism: Classical Strecker mechanism: (1) Nucleophilic addition of NH₃ to the carbonyl of isobutyraldehyde forms a hemiaminal (carbinolamine). (2) Dehydration yields an imine (Schiff base), 2-methylpropan-1-imine. (3) Nucleophilic addition of HCN to the imine carbon yields α-aminoisovaleronitrile. Montmorillonite concentrates reactants on its surface and provides acid-base catalysis for each step.
reactions.reasoning: The Strecker synthesis is one of the best-established prebiotic amino acid synthesis routes (Miller, 1957; Strecker, 1850). Isobutyraldehyde Strecker synthesis specifically produces valine precursors (Miller & Urey, 1959; Schlesinger & Miller, 1983). Montmorillonite clays catalyze Strecker reactions by concentrating reactants and providing acid-base sites (Ferris, 2005). The reaction proceeds efficiently under mild aqueous conditions with excess NH₃ and HCN.

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis step 2: hydrolysis of aminonitrile to valine

conditions.other: mildly acidic to neutral pH, prolonged aqueous conditions or heating

reactions.mechanism: Acid-catalyzed hydrolysis of the nitrile group in α-aminoisovaleronitrile. The nitrile is first hydrated to an amide (α-aminoisovaleramide), then the amide undergoes further hydrolysis to yield the carboxylic acid (valine) with release of NH₃. Under prebiotic conditions, this occurs over extended time at ambient temperature or accelerated by mild heating.
reactions.reasoning: Nitrile hydrolysis to carboxylic acids is spontaneous in aqueous solution, especially under mildly acidic conditions or elevated temperatures (Schlesinger & Miller, 1983). The hydrolysis of Strecker aminonitriles to amino acids was demonstrated in the original Miller-Urey experiments (Miller, 1957). The reaction is thermodynamically favorable (ΔG < 0) and is accelerated at 350-370K, making geothermal pools ideal environments.

reactions.name: Reductive carboxylation of acetaldehyde to pyruvate on native iron

conditions.other: mildly alkaline, CO₂ saturated fluid

reactions.mechanism: CO₂ fixation onto acetaldehyde via reductive carboxylation on native iron surface. Fe⁰ provides electrons for the activation of CO₂. The activated CO₂ species attacks the carbonyl carbon of acetaldehyde, forming a C3 α-keto acid (pyruvate) through a mechanism analogous to enzymatic pyruvate synthase (CO₂ + 2e⁻ + acetaldehyde → pyruvate).
reactions.reasoning: Varma et al. (2018, Nature Ecology & Evolution) demonstrated that native iron (Fe⁰) promotes CO₂ fixation to generate α-keto acids including pyruvate from acetaldehyde + CO₂ under hydrothermal conditions. This is a key finding establishing a non-enzymatic analog of the reductive acetyl-CoA pathway and reductive TCA cycle reactions. Pyruvate yields of ~1-5% were observed at 100°C.

reactions.name: Reductive carboxylation of isobutyraldehyde to α-ketoisovalerate on native iron

conditions.other: mildly alkaline, CO₂ saturated, analogous to Varma et al. conditions

reactions.mechanism: CO₂ fixation onto isobutyraldehyde via reductive carboxylation on native iron surface, directly analogous to pyruvate formation from acetaldehyde (rxn_012). Fe⁰ activates CO₂, which attacks the carbonyl carbon of isobutyraldehyde, forming the C5 α-keto acid (α-ketoisovalerate). The branched chain of isobutyraldehyde is preserved in the product.
reactions.reasoning: By direct analogy with Varma et al. (2018), who showed Fe⁰-mediated CO₂ fixation onto aldehydes to generate α-keto acids, isobutyraldehyde + CO₂ → α-ketoisovalerate is a logical extension. Muchowska et al. (2019, Nature) demonstrated that Fe⁰ promotes a wide range of α-keto acid syntheses from aldehydes + CO₂, supporting the generality of this reductive carboxylation mechanism. This avoids the problematic acetolactate pathway entirely.

reactions.name: Reductive amination of α-ketoisovalerate to valine on greigite

conditions.other: NH₃-rich alkaline vent fluid, pH 9-11

reactions.mechanism: Reductive amination on mineral surface: (1) NH₃ attacks the keto group of α-ketoisovalerate forming a carbinolamine. (2) Dehydration yields an imine (Schiff base). (3) Reduction of the C=N double bond by H₂ (or surface-bound hydride from FeS) yields valine. FeS/Fe₃S₄ surfaces facilitate all three steps.
reactions.reasoning: Huber & Wächtershäuser (2003) demonstrated reductive amination of α-keto acids to amino acids on FeS/NiS surfaces under simulated hydrothermal conditions. Wang et al. (2005) showed that greigite and other iron sulfides promote reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine. By extension, α-ketoisovalerate undergoes analogous reductive amination to valine. The reaction is thermodynamically favorable under reducing hydrothermal conditions with excess NH₃.

reactions.name: Reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine on greigite

conditions.other: NH₃-rich alkaline vent fluid

reactions.mechanism: Reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine: NH₃ attacks the keto carbonyl of pyruvate, dehydration gives pyruvate imine, and reduction on greigite surface yields alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Huber & Wächtershäuser (2003) demonstrated reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine on metal sulfide surfaces. Wang et al. (2005) confirmed this with greigite catalysis. Alanine is one of the most readily synthesized amino acids via reductive amination, providing the amino donor for subsequent transamination to valine.

reactions.name: Transamination of α-ketoisovalerate with alanine to form valine

conditions.catalyst: Cu²⁺ or Fe²⁺ ions as Lewis acid catalyst
conditions.other: concentrated pool, Cu²⁺/Fe²⁺ ~1-10 mM, pH 5-8

reactions.mechanism: Metal-ion-catalyzed transamination. Cu²⁺ or Fe²⁺ coordinates both the amino group of alanine and the keto group of α-ketoisovalerate, facilitating proton transfer and amino group exchange through a geminal diamine intermediate. Alanine donates its amino group to α-ketoisovalerate, yielding valine and regenerating pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: Meinert et al. (2016) and Transamination reactions have been shown to proceed abiotically in the presence of metal ions, especially Cu²⁺ (Crespo-Hernandez et al.). Nakada & Hagiwara (1966) demonstrated non-enzymatic transamination between amino acids and α-keto acids catalyzed by metal ions. Pyruvate regeneration creates a catalytic cycle, consistent with proto-metabolic network concepts (Smith & Morowitz, 2004). This provides a third independent route to valine.

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis of glycine from formaldehyde

conditions.other: evaporitic pool, concentrated reactants

reactions.mechanism: Strecker synthesis using formaldehyde as the aldehyde: HCHO + NH₃ → methyleneamine, then + HCN → aminoacetonitrile, then hydrolysis → glycine. The simplest Strecker synthesis producing the simplest amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Glycine is the most abundant amino acid in Miller-Urey experiments and the simplest Strecker product (Miller, 1957). Its synthesis from HCHO + HCN + NH₃ is the archetypal Strecker reaction and is exceptionally well-supported experimentally. Glycine serves as an alternative amino group donor for transamination reactions.

reactions.name: Transamination of α-ketoisovalerate with glycine to form valine

conditions.other: concentrated pool, metal ions present

reactions.mechanism: Metal-ion-catalyzed transamination between glycine (amino donor) and α-ketoisovalerate (keto acceptor). The glycine amino group is transferred to the keto position of α-ketoisovalerate, producing valine and glyoxylate as the keto acid byproduct.
reactions.reasoning: Analogous to rxn_016 but using glycine as the amino donor. Non-enzymatic transamination with glycine has been demonstrated under prebiotic conditions (Nakada & Hagiwara, 1966). Using glycine provides an alternative amino group source independent of the alanine/pyruvate cycle, increasing network robustness.

reactions.name: Cyanohydrin formation from isobutyraldehyde with HCN

conditions.other: mildly basic pH favors CN⁻ nucleophile

reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic addition of cyanide (CN⁻) to the carbonyl carbon of isobutyraldehyde to form the cyanohydrin (2-hydroxy-3-methylbutanenitrile). The reaction proceeds under mild conditions in aqueous solution, favored at pH 8-9 where CN⁻ concentration is appreciable.
reactions.reasoning: Cyanohydrin formation is the first mechanistic step of the Strecker synthesis when performed without initial imine formation (Strecker, 1850). The cyanohydrin can proceed to valine via two distinct routes: (1) ammonolysis followed by hydrolysis (Strecker completion), or (2) hydrolysis to α-hydroxy acid then conversion to α-keto acid. This branching point increases pathway diversity.

reactions.name: Cyanohydrin ammonolysis and hydrolysis to valine

conditions.other: prolonged heating in NH₃-rich solution, geothermal pool

reactions.mechanism: The cyanohydrin undergoes ammonolysis: NH₃ displaces the hydroxyl group at the alpha carbon to give α-aminoisovaleronitrile (mol_012 equivalent), followed by nitrile hydrolysis (nitrile → amide → carboxylic acid) to yield valine. The two-step process of SN2 ammonolysis + hydrolysis proceeds thermally in NH₃-rich geothermal pool conditions.
reactions.reasoning: The cyanohydrin-to-amino-acid route is a variant of the Strecker synthesis documented by Commeyras et al. (2002) and studied in the context of α-amino acid synthesis in prebiotic chemistry. In concentrated NH₃ solutions under mild heating, the hydroxyl group is displaced by NH₃, and subsequent hydrolysis converts the nitrile to the carboxylic acid. This provides an alternative to the direct imine-HCN Strecker route.

reactions.name: Thermal decarboxylation of pyruvate to acetaldehyde

conditions.other: wet-dry cycling concentrates pyruvate, thermal decarboxylation during dry phase

reactions.mechanism: Thermal decarboxylation of pyruvate: CH₃COCOO⁻ → CH₃CHO + CO₂. The β-keto acid motif makes decarboxylation facile. Loss of CO₂ from the carboxylate group yields acetaldehyde. Metal ions (Fe²⁺, Mn²⁺) on clay surfaces lower the activation energy.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate decarboxylation is thermodynamically favorable and well-documented under prebiotic conditions (Cooper et al., 2011). Thermal decarboxylation of pyruvate occurs readily at 60-100°C in aqueous solution (Sato & Itoh, 2006). This creates a bidirectional connection between pyruvate and acetaldehyde, enabling the pyruvate ↔ acetaldehyde cycle that connects the transamination and FTT branches of the network.

pathways.name: Primary Strecker via aldol-derived isobutyraldehyde
pathways.description: The strongest and best-supported pathway. Hydrothermal CO₂ → CO via magnetite, then FTT gives formaldehyde and propanal. Surface aldol condensation of propanal + formaldehyde → methacrolein → isobutyraldehyde. Strecker synthesis with HCN + NH₃ gives aminonitrile, hydrolysis yields valine.

pathways.name: Strecker via formate-derived formaldehyde and FTT propanal
pathways.description: Alternative formaldehyde source via greigite-catalyzed CO₂ → formate, then Fe⁰ reduction to formaldehyde. Propanal from FTT. Aldol → isobutyraldehyde → Strecker → valine. Uses two independent hydrothermal C1 pathways.

pathways.name: Strecker via direct FTT isobutyraldehyde
pathways.description: Shortest pathway. Hydrothermal FTT directly produces branched isobutyraldehyde (low yield). Surface Strecker with HCN + NH₃ → aminonitrile → valine. Only 4 reaction steps, minimal accumulated uncertainty per step but low initial yield.

pathways.name: Reductive amination via aldol isobutyraldehyde carboxylation
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO₂ → CO → FTT aldehydes. Surface aldol gives isobutyraldehyde. Hydrothermal Fe⁰-mediated carboxylation gives α-ketoisovalerate (Varma et al. analog). Reductive amination on greigite yields valine.

pathways.name: Reductive amination via direct FTT isobutyraldehyde carboxylation
pathways.description: FTT directly produces isobutyraldehyde. Fe⁰-mediated carboxylation with CO₂ gives α-ketoisovalerate. Reductive amination on greigite yields valine. Short pathway, 4 steps.

pathways.name: Transamination with alanine via aldol route
pathways.description: Convergent pathway requiring both α-ketoisovalerate (from aldol-derived isobutyraldehyde + CO₂ carboxylation) and alanine (from FTT acetaldehyde + CO₂ → pyruvate → reductive amination). Transamination yields valine and regenerates pyruvate in a catalytic cycle.

pathways.name: Transamination with glycine
pathways.description: α-Ketoisovalerate from any route (P4/P5) combined with glycine from Strecker synthesis of formaldehyde. Transamination yields valine. Uses glycine as alternative amino donor independent of pyruvate/alanine cycle.

pathways.name: Cyanohydrin route to valine
pathways.description: Isobutyraldehyde (from aldol or FTT) reacts with HCN to form cyanohydrin intermediate. Subsequent ammonolysis and hydrolysis yields valine. Mechanistic variant of Strecker that proceeds through a distinct cyanohydrin intermediate.

pathways.name: Formate-formaldehyde-HCN integrated Strecker with FTT isobutyraldehyde
pathways.description: Uses the formate → formaldehyde → HCN branch for cyanide source, combined with direct FTT isobutyraldehyde. Integrates both hydrothermal CO₂ fixation pathways into a single route to valine via Strecker.

pathways.name: Pyruvate decarboxylation recycling with transamination
pathways.description: Demonstrates the full proto-metabolic cycle. FTT acetaldehyde → pyruvate (Fe⁰ carboxylation) → alanine (reductive amination). Separately, pyruvate decarboxylation regenerates acetaldehyde. α-Ketoisovalerate from FTT isobutyraldehyde carboxylation. Transamination of α-KIV + alanine → valine + pyruvate, closing the cycle.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The Strecker route via aldol-derived isobutyraldehyde is a well-conceived pathway. The main liabilities are rxn_006 (photochemical HCN from formaldehyde + NH₃ is non-standard; prebiotic HCN is typically produced atmospherically from CH₄/N₂/NH₃ mixtures) and rxn_008 (pyrite is a weak hydrogenation catalyst for the methacrolein reduction). The Strecker terminal steps (rxn_010, rxn_011) are robust and well-supported by decades of literature.
suggestions: Replace rxn_006 with atmospheric electric-discharge or UV-photolysis HCN production from NH₃/CH₄/N₂
Consider FeS or Ni-Fe alloys instead of pyrite for methacrolein reduction (rxn_008)
Alternatively, treat HCN as a prebiotically available starting material

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Longest Strecker variant that routes through formate as an alternative formaldehyde source. The formate-to-formaldehyde reduction on native iron (rxn_004) adds a plausible but poorly characterized step. Same HCN and methacrolein reduction issues as P1. The added length without proportional gain in robustness makes this less attractive than P1.
suggestions: Provide experimental support for formate-to-formaldehyde reduction yields
Shorten by using rxn_003 directly

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Short and elegant but critically dependent on rxn_009 (direct FTT synthesis of branched C4 aldehyde). FTT chemistry overwhelmingly favors linear products; branched isobutyraldehyde would be a very minor product at best. Combined with the HCN synthesis issue, two of five steps are problematic.
suggestions: Cite any experimental evidence for branched FTT aldehydes
This pathway should be considered supplementary, not primary

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The strongest pathway in the network. Reductive amination avoids HCN entirely, removing a major vulnerability. The Varma-type reductive carboxylation (rxn_013) is speculative for branched substrates but supported by analogy. The aldol condensation route to isobutyraldehyde is chemically more defensible than direct FTT branching. The final reductive amination on greigite (rxn_014) is well-supported by Wang et al.-type work. Weakest links are rxn_008 (methacrolein reduction) and rxn_013 (carboxylation selectivity).
suggestions: Specify temperature/pressure for rxn_013 and cite Varma et al. directly
Consider alternative minerals (awaruite, Ni-Fe) for methacrolein reduction

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Shortest pathway (4 steps) but relies on the most speculative step in the network (rxn_009, direct FTT to isobutyraldehyde). The reductive amination terminus is strong, but the foundation is weak. Would be excellent if branched FTT products could be experimentally validated.
suggestions: Provide yield data for branched FTT products
Better as a supplementary shortcut than a primary route

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Transamination with alanine is biologically relevant but prebiotically challenging. rxn_016 requires pyridoxal phosphate or an equivalent prebiotic cofactor, which is not specified. The pathway is long (9 steps) and accumulates uncertainties. The parallel synthesis of alanine and α-ketoisovalerate is a nice convergent feature, but the transamination step itself is the bottleneck.
suggestions: Specify a prebiotic transamination catalyst (e.g., pyridoxal, metal ions, or mineral surfaces)
Consider citing Barge et al. or other work on mineral-catalyzed transamination

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Transamination with glycine is elegant in concept—glycine is the simplest amino acid and likely the most abundant prebiotically. However, the same cofactor problem applies to rxn_018. Additionally, this pathway combines three problematic reactions (rxn_006, rxn_009, rxn_018).
suggestions: Replace rxn_009 with aldol route
Specify transamination catalyst

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The cyanohydrin route is mechanistically problematic. rxn_020 (cyanohydrin + NH₃ → amino acid) reverses the standard Strecker order: in the conventional mechanism, the amine attacks the aldehyde first to form an imine, then HCN adds. When cyanohydrin forms first, ammonolysis would need to displace the hydroxyl—this is thermodynamically unfavorable and would more likely yield α-hydroxy acids (not amino acids) under hydrolysis.
suggestions: Reconsider mechanism: cyanohydrin hydrolysis gives α-hydroxy acids, not amino acids
Merge rxn_019+rxn_020 into the standard Strecker sequence (rxn_010+rxn_011) instead

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Integrates formate-derived formaldehyde with direct FTT isobutyraldehyde. Combines weaknesses of both (rxn_004 uncertain yields, rxn_009 speculative branching, rxn_006 questionable HCN synthesis). The Strecker terminus remains strong.
suggestions: Use aldol route for isobutyraldehyde instead of rxn_009

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The pyruvate recycling loop (rxn_021 decarboxylation → rxn_012 re-carboxylation) is thermodynamically wasteful and prebiotically unrealistic as a productive cycle. The alanine-to-valine transamination still requires unspecified cofactors. The network connectivity is interesting but the pathway efficiency is low.
suggestions: Remove the decarboxylation-recarboxylation loop as it adds no net value
Focus on direct pyruvate synthesis rather than recycling

evaluation.network_feedback: This is a well-designed network with excellent topology—19 molecules, 21 reactions, 5 genuine hub molecules, and 10 pathways providing good redundancy. The convergence architecture is strong, with isobutyraldehyde and α-ketoisovalerate serving as effective bridge hubs between hydrothermal/surface and biochemical environments. The three terminal strategies (Strecker, reductive amination, transamination) appropriately diversify the route portfolio. Key strengths: (1) the reductive amination pathway P4 avoids the problematic HCN synthesis entirely and reaches valine through well-supported chemistry; (2) hub molecules create genuine crosslinks rather than mere serial intermediates; (3) mineral catalysts are specified throughout. Key weaknesses: (1) rxn_006 (HCN from CH₂O + NH₃) is not the standard prebiotic HCN source—this undermines all Strecker pathways; (2) rxn_009 (direct FTT branched aldehyde) is speculative—FTT strongly favors linear chains; (3) transamination reactions (rxn_016, rxn_018) lack cofactor specification; (4) rxn_020 (cyanohydrin ammonolysis) is mechanistically flawed. Despite these issues, the network's redundancy means viable routes exist even when weaker reactions are discounted. P4 (reductive amination via aldol-derived isobutyraldehyde) stands as a chemically defensible route to valine with all steps grounded in plausible prebiotic chemistry.
